rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe seborrhoeic dermatitis with anterior blepharitis is a benign, common, chronic-relapsing eruption that is telehealth-recognisable and topically manageable. On the patient-obtainable data the sight-threat screen is negative (no deep eye pain, no photophobia, no vision change), the patient is afebrile and systemically well with no spreading hot periorbital swelling, and there are no grouped vesicles or punched-out erosions - so neither an eye emergency nor systemic infection is indicated, and baseline escalation to urgent same-day/ED (T3-T5) or systemic antibiotics/antivirals would be over-triage/over-treatment. However, because Breath-Ezy cannot examine the eye or cornea remotely and the flare is periocular, a prompt (within 24-48 h) in-person GP look at the eye is prudent - so the baseline is T2 (prompt GP review) rather than routine-only, with a prominent, mandatory eye safety-net. A competent recognise-plus-topical-treatment-plus-eye-safety-net answer with routine review (T1) is also acceptable; the scored defects are over-triage to ED/ambulance and under-triage that dismisses the eye symptoms with no screen or safety-net.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any change in actual vision (blurring or loss), severe deep pain inside the eye, or pain on looking at light (photophobia)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department / urgent eye service the same day (within hours)
escalation_edges.rationale: These are the hallmarks of sight-threatening corneal / herpetic keratitis, which needs urgent same-day ophthalmology; steroids to the eye can worsen it.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your eyesight changes, or you get severe pain deep inside the eye, or bright light becomes painful, stop the steroid cream and go to an emergency department or urgent eye clinic that same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The skin around the eye becomes hot, tensely swollen and spreads quickly, the eye is pushed forward or hurts to move, or you develop a fever and feel unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the nearest emergency department the same day (within hours)
escalation_edges.rationale: Hot spreading periorbital swelling, painful/restricted eye movements, proptosis or fever suggest periorbital progressing to orbital cellulitis - a sight- and life-threatening emergency needing urgent IV antibiotics and imaging.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin around your eye turns hot and tightly swollen and spreads fast, your eye starts to bulge or hurts to move, or you spike a fever and feel unwell, go to the emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Crops of small clear blisters, or round punched-out sores/ulcers, appear on the face - especially near the eye - or the rash rapidly worsens with feeling unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent same-day in-person / emergency assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: Grouped vesicles or monomorphic punched-out erosions suggest eczema herpeticum (HSV superinfection), a dermatological emergency needing systemic aciclovir and, with ocular involvement, urgent ophthalmology.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you notice clusters of little clear blisters or round punched-out sores on your face, especially near the eye, or the rash suddenly gets much worse and you feel unwell, get seen urgently the same day - this needs antiviral treatment, not just the cream.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The flare is not settling within 5-7 days of treatment, or keeps relapsing
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent or relapsing seborrhoeic dermatitis warrants a routine GP review to confirm the diagnosis, review technique and plan long-term maintenance; it is not an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the itch and redness haven't clearly improved within about a week, or it keeps coming back, book a routine appointment with your GP to review the treatment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Periocular involvement (anterior blepharitis, mild conjunctival injection) that telehealth cannot examine
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to arrange a prompt in-person eye look (T2, within 24-48 h) and give a prominent mandatory eye safety-net; but a negative patient-obtainable sight-threat screen does NOT justify baseline escalation to urgent same-day/ED. Keep all topical steroid away from the eyelids and eye.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Severe psychosocial distress, low mood and functional impairment (housebound, off work)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Prioritise rapid symptom control and clear reassurance about the benign, treatable nature of the condition, set the 3-5 day improvement expectation, and offer GP follow-up for mood and the impact on work - the distress is a reason for prompt effective treatment and support, not for emergency escalation.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a benign, common skin condition that flares with stress and cold weather and settles with treatment. Use the medicated (ketoconazole) shampoo on your scalp and face every 2-3 days, apply a thin smear of mild hydrocortisone 1% cream to the red facial patches twice a day for no more than 5-7 days (keep it well away from your eyes), and gently clean the eyelid crusts with diluted baby shampoo on a cotton bud each morning and night. Because we can't examine your eye over a screen, arrange to be seen in person by your GP in the next day or two to check the eye is safe. The itch and redness should improve within 3-5 days.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care with a prompt (24-48 h) in-person GP review to examine the eye; emergency department for the eye warning signs below
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If any of the eye warning signs appear after hours (vision change, severe eye pain, pain looking at light, hot spreading swelling around the eye, fever, or clusters of blisters / punched-out sores), go to the nearest emergency department or call healthdirect on 1800 022 222 - do not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Severe seborrhoeic dermatitis with acute anterior blepharitis, flare on a stress/cold trigger; sight-threat and systemic red-flag screen negative on patient-obtainable data (no deep eye pain/photophobia/vision change, afebrile, no spreading periorbital swelling, no vesicles/punched-out erosions). Started ketoconazole 2% shampoo, short-course hydrocortisone 1% to the face (away from eyes) and lid hygiene; prompt in-person eye look requested; explicit eye safety-net given; routine review ~7 days for resolution and maintenance.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as 'just dandruff', giving self-care with no eye screen and no safety-net for the sight-threatening periocular mimics.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An early herpetic keratitis or periorbital cellulitis, unscreened and un-safety-netted, progresses over the following day to corneal scarring / orbital cellulitis and permanent visual loss because the patient was told it was harmless.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising the benign eruption but failing to screen for or explicitly safety-net the eye red flags, or wrongly directing steroid onto the eyelids.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Directing an afebrile patient with a negative sight-threat red-flag screen to urgent same-day care / ED / ambulance, or prescribing systemic antibiotics or antivirals - unnecessary alarm, drug exposure and emergency-resource use driven by the alarming periocular presentation and distress. This is the primary scored defect for this case.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot examine the eye or cornea (no slit-lamp, fluorescein, formal acuity, or assessment of eye movements/proptosis), so a sight-threatening herpetic keratitis or an early periorbital cellulitis cannot be 100% excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Weight the patient-obtainable data (no deep eye pain/photophobia/vision change, afebrile, no spreading periorbital swelling, no vesicles) toward the benign diagnosis and manage conservatively, WITH a prominent, mandatory eye safety-net and a prompt (24-48 h) in-person eye look, keeping all steroid away from the eyes. Do not reflexively escalate to ED or prescribe antibiotics/antivirals for the benign eruption.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either (a) escalating to ED / prescribing systemic antibiotics or antivirals 'just in case' for an afebrile patient with a negative sight-threat screen - over-triage and over-treatment; or (b) reassuring as benign with no eye screen and no safety-net, missing a genuine herpetic keratitis or periorbital cellulitis.
